claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child in local authority care for 12 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05); Child in local authority care for 12 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
circumstances: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
Child in local authority care for 12 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions.

Let's start with: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have one child - my son Alex. He's 8 years old. I'd like to know if I can claim Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I can see you have one child, Alex, who is 8 years old.

To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, I need to ask you a few questions.

**First, do you currently live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great! And my next question is: **Does Alex live with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't live with me at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know. Since Alex doesn't live with you currently, I need to understand your situation a bit better.

**Do you contribute financially towards Alex's upkeep, such as for food, clothes, pocket money, or accommodation costs? And if so, approximately how much do you contribute per month or week?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do help out a bit with his expenses - things like clothes and bits and bobs he needs. I contribute about £10 a week.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. That's helpful to know.

**Can you tell me who Alex currently lives with? For example, does he live with his other parent, a relative, or someone else?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He's currently in local authority care - he's been in a children's home for a couple of months now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for sharing that. I understand this must be a difficult situation.

**Just to clarify, does Alex spend any regular time at home with you? For example, does he come home for visits, and if so, how often and for how long?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't come home for visits at the moment. He stays at the children's home full-time.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Based on what you've told me, I need to inform you of the eligibility outcome:

Since Alex has been in local authority care for a couple of months (which is more than 8 weeks) and he doesn't spend at least 24 hours a week at home with you, you would not normally be eligible for Child Benefit for him under the standard rules. While you are contributing £10 per week towards his upkeep, when a child is in care for more than 8 weeks, the exception that allows continued Child Benefit payments typically requires the child to spend at least 24 hours a week at home, which isn't happening in your case.

However, given the complexity of situations involving children in care, I would strongly recommend contacting the Child Benefit Office directly to discuss your specific circumstances, as there may be other factors they can consider.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'm sorry to deliver this outcome, but based on the current circumstances, you would not be eligible for Child Benefit for Alex. If your situation changes in the future - for example, if Alex begins spending regular time at home with you - your eligibility could be reassessed. I wish you and Alex all the best.

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old and in local authority care. According to the rules, Child Benefit payments are affected when a child goes into care for more than 8 weeks. Alex has been in a children's home for a couple of months (more than 8 weeks). The claimant does not live with Alex, and Alex does not spend regular time at home. While the claimant contributes £10 per week towards Alex's upkeep, the exception for children in care requires the child to spend at least 24 hours a week at home with the claimant. Since Alex stays at the children's home full-time and does not come home for visits, this exception does not apply. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK and has one child, Alex (age 8), who has been in local authority care for a couple of months. Alex does not live with the claimant and does not spend any regular time at home. While the claimant contributes £10 per week towards Alex's expenses, the rules state that after 8 weeks in care, Child Benefit eligibility ends unless the child spends at least 24 hours a week at home, which is not the case here. The claimant is therefore not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Alex has been in a children's home for a couple of months (more than 8 weeks). The claimant does not live with Alex, and Alex does not spend regular time at home. While the claimant contributes £10 per week towards Alex's upkeep, the exception for children in care requires the child to spend at least 24 hours a week at home with the claimant. Since Alex stays at the children's home full-time and does not come home for visits, this exception does not apply. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK and has one child, Alex (age 8), who has been in local authority care for a couple of months. Alex does not live with the claimant and does not spend any regular time at home. While the claimant contributes £10 per week towards Alex's expenses, the rules state that after 8 weeks in care, Child Benefit eligibility ends unless the child spends at least 24 hours a week at home, which is not the case here. The claimant is therefore not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
